solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public char kthCharacter(long k, int[] operations) {
        long len = 1;
        char[] word = new char[1];
        word[0] = 'a';
        
        for (int op : operations) {
            if (op == 0) {
                // Append a copy of word to itself
                len *= 2;
                char[] newWord = new char[(int) len];
                System.arraycopy(word, 0, newWord, 0, word.length);
                System.arraycopy(word, 0, newWord, word.length, word.length);
                word = newWord;
            } else {
                // Generate a new string by changing each character to its next character
                len *= 2;
                char[] newWord = new char[(int) len];
                System.arraycopy(word, 0, newWord, 0, word.length);
                for (int i = 0; i < word.length; i++) {
                    char c = word[i];
                    newWord[word.length + i] = (char) ((c - 'a' + 1) % 26 + 'a');
                }
                word = newWord;
            }
        }
        
        return word[(int) k - 1];
    }
}
```
efficiency: MEDIUM
time complexity: O(N * L), where N is the number of operations and L is the length of the word after each operation
space complexity: O(L), where L is the length of the word after all operations
energy implications: MEDIUM
explanation: The solution simulates the operations directly by appending either a copy of the current string or a transformed version of it. The key strength is its clarity and correctness for small inputs, but it is inefficient for large k due to the exponential growth of the string length. The main weakness is the high memory usage and time complexity for large operations.
